text: Polarized training produces moderately greater improvements in time-trial performance compared to threshold training in endurance athletes.
population: trained endurance athletes (>2 years experience, VO2max >50 ml/kg/min)

text: Polarized training distributes intensity predominantly in low and high zones while minimizing time in the moderate/threshold zone, contrasting with threshold-focused models that emphasize sustained moderate-intensity work.

The evidence base comparing polarized vs. threshold training is limited, with only 4 randomized controlled trials meeting inclusion criteria and study quality scores of 4-5 out of 10 on the PEDro Scale.
population: trained endurance athletes (>2 years experience, VO2max >50 ml/kg/min)

text: Polarized training may improve VO2max/VO2peak, time-to-exhaustion, and exercise economy compared to threshold training, though evidence varies across these outcomes.
population: trained endurance athletes (>2 years experience, VO2max >50 ml/kg/min)

text: For endurance athletes with VO2max above 50 ml/kg/min and more than 2 years of training experience, a polarized intensity distribution should be preferred over a threshold-dominant distribution for optimizing race performance.
population: trained endurance athletes (>2 years experience, VO2max >50 ml/kg/min)
